1.grade_content: 3.5.1 Clarification of Grade 1 content MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 CAPS 93 94 Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting. Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting. Count objects Count out objects reliably to 10. Count out objects reliably to 50. FOCUS FOR TERM 1 1.1 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS TOPICS Counting on The cardinality principle Working with written texts • • • count forward and backwards; count actions; count in sequence on a number line; develop an awareness of the size of numbers by ordering and comparing them; and estimate and predict. • • • • • CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) It is important that learners understand that the last number named represents the last object counted in the group. They must know and understand that the last number named indicates the amount in the set or the cardinality of the set. Not all learners who can count orally and say the number names in sequence will attach meaning to their counts. They might skip numbers and say that there are four counters when there are actually five counters. Learners then count each object and match number names to sets of objects. This involves touching and moving the object and saying the number name. Before learners count objects they need opportunities to practise counting orally. Learners need to have an oral list of number names in order: one, two, and three until 20. Encourage learners to say number rhymes and play games that reinforce the oral counting. This ability to count orally or rote count is important to develop the knowledge of number names and also a sense of the rhythm/pattern within numbers. Counting objects count grouped and ungrouped objects; • Counting in Term 1 is focussed on developing learner’s counting skills. The development of counting skills allows them to: Counting all • Counting helps learners to develop an awareness of the size of numbers and lays the basis for calculating with whole numbers. During the first term learners develop the following skills: SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES (in lessons of 1 hour 24 minutes) DURATION MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 CAPS Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting. Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting. Count objects Count out objects reliably to 10. Count out objects reliably to 50. FOCUS FOR TERM 1 1.1 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS TOPICS Bring me six pencils Count six buttons • • three counters, arranged in any way one to six, from the arrangement of dots on a die • • to associate number words with a collection of objects; to build a mental picture of what a number means i.e. how big it is; and that the number name of the last object counted represents the total number of objects in the group. • • • Fundamental number concepts are developed by counting real objects. Learners learn: Therefore: This is also known as subitising. five, as the number of fingers on one hand • Example, recognising It is important that when counting concrete objects, learners recognise a small number of objects without counting. The position or arrangement of the stars is different but the number of stars on each card is exactly the same. Learners will begin to realise when counting the number of objects, that the number is not affected by their size or position. One could ask: how many stars on each card? Conservation Count six counters • Example: It is important that learners count the same number of different objects. SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES (in lessons of 1 hour 24 minutes) DURATION MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 95 96 Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting. Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting. Count objects Count out objects reliably to 10. Count out objects reliably to 50. FOCUS FOR TERM 1 1.1 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS TOPICS Count these bottle tops without touching them. Is there the same number of each? How many crayons do you think there are? Are there more or fewer than you thought? How do you know that you have that number? How do you know that you counted every crayon? How could you check your answer? • • • • • • • CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) Matchsticks Ice cream sticks Peach pips Beans Bottle tops • • • • • Example: It is useful to build up a collection of different kinds of objects that learners can use as counters. Resources: Counting-on is a far more efficient counting strategy than counting all and learners will use counting on when they calculate. Ask learners to count out seven objects and then, starting from the seven objects, count out three more until they have 10 objects. Once learners can confidently count all the objects starting from one, they should count on from a collection they already have. Example: Counting on How many? Learners need to be given opportunities to count illustrations of objects. Example: Moving to written texts Rearrange the sticks and count again. Is the number still the same? Count 10 counting sticks. Arrange them in a line. • • Instructions and questions to support the counting of objects SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES (in lessons of 1 hour 24 minutes) DURATION MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 CAPS 10s from any multiple of 10 between 0 and 100 5s from any multiple of 5 between 0 and 100 2s from any multiple of 2 between 0 and 100 • • • Count forwards in Count forwards and backwards in ones from any number between 0 and 100 1.2 Count forwards and backwards REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS TOPICS Count forwards and backwards in Ones from any number between 1 and 20 FOCUS FOR TERM 1 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS Count from four to 10 Count from two to eight. How many numbers did you count? • • An abacus A number line • • Counting beyond ten might require the learners to say the counting sequence after the teacher. It is also important to encourage learners to start counting at any number. Starting at 8 is far more demanding than starting at 1. Learners will use this skill when they count on in addition. Counting to 20 Counting backwards is a difficult skill for learners and frequent practice is necessary. Start counting backwards from a number when learners are familiar with, 2, and build this up as you focus on each higher number. Counting backwards Initially a number line with all the numbers represented, and then a number line with some numbers not represented, can be used. This means that learners will have to know the numbers in sequence to fill in the gaps. Counting beads • Learners need to be presented with visual images of numbers in sequence. The following visual images can be used: Learners can get bored with verbal counting and they need to do it in different ways, otherwise they are simply chanting. Rote counting needs to be supported by reading number symbols and counting objects. Initially learners will start counting to ten and practising the number names in sequence. Counting to ten Start at two and count to eight • Counting forwards and backwards can be done with the whole class. Make sure that learners are not just chanting meaninglessly. In the focus groups and the independent work the following activities can be done to add meaning to the counting: In Term 1 it is expected that learners only count forwards and backwards in ones till 20. Counting orally or doing verbal counting (rote counting) is an important step in reciting the number names in order. If learners are able to do this, it does not mean that they have an understanding of the size of the numbers they are saying. There is no relationship between the number and the quantity or size of the numbers. Counting forwards in ones SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES (in lessons of…

3.grade_content: 3.5.3 Clarification of Grade 3 content MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) CAPS Count objects 1.1 TOPICS Estimate and count reliably to at least objects. The strategy of grouping is encouraged. Counting concrete Objects REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS Group to at least objects to estimate and count reliably Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting • • FOCUS FOR TERM 1 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS Place value cards • Each group shows a hundred. Example: The focus is on grouping the objects. Learners should have a strong sense that it is better, more efficient and quicker to count in groups of tens, twenties, fifties and hundreds than in ones. They start counting in hundreds, forwards and backwards during the first term. To support the rote counting, learners can organise the objects in groups of 100s. During the first term learners practise and consolidate counting objects to Dienes blocks • During Grade learners continue to count out everyday objects. The number range will increase to by the end of the year. This means that careful consideration needs to be given to the kind of apparatus used: SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES (in lessons of 1 hour 24 minutes) DURATION MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 341 342 Count objects 1.1 TOPICS Estimate and count reliably to at least objects. The strategy of grouping is encouraged. Counting concrete Objects REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS Group to at least objects to estimate and count reliably Give a reasonable estimate of a number of objects that can be checked by counting • • FOCUS FOR TERM 4 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) understanding the value of a digit; breaking down and building up numbers; and reading number symbols. • • • The focus of these kinds of activities is not only on counting objects. At the same time learners are: Learners can use the place value cards to show the value of each digit. The value of these base blocks are_____________ Learners should be able to complete the following statement: During this term learners can represent numbers using the Dienes blocks or base ten blocks. Learners used these apparatus in Grade 2. Learners can count the hundreds together because they have done so when counting objects grouped in hundreds. Learners then count the tens (they have done so since Grade 1) and then the ones. Learners need to understand why they are spending their time counting objects. The links need to be made explicit. 362 + 527 = (300 + 60 + 2) + (500 + 20 + 7). Example: Counting objects by breaking up objects into groups of ten, 20s, 50s or even hundreds will allow learners to break up numbers when adding or subtracting. To add 362 + 527, learners can break up the number into place value parts. i.e. During this term and for the rest of the year learners need to be made aware of how the counting of objects will help in calculations. SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES (in lessons of 1 hour 24 minutes) DURATION MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 CAPS Count forwards and backwards 1.2 TOPICS Count forwards and backwards in: the intervals specified in Grade 2 with increased number ranges 100s to at least 500 • • the intervals specified in grade 2 with increased number ranges from any given number in 20s, 25s, 50s,100s to at least 1 000 • • FOCUS FOR TERM 4 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS Count forwards and backwards in: REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS Number grids • 2 fours are 8 3 fours are 12 • • The skip counting also supports understanding multiplication and will help learners when they complete number sequences. 1 four is 4 • The skip counting needs to be linked to the times tables. Counting in 4s will help learners when they say: Number lines Dienes blocks • • Counting beads • Learners count in 100s for the first time. They do this to 500. Learners need supporting base ten apparatus such as: What is different from Grade 2? SOME CLARIFICATION NOTES OR TEACHING GUIDELINES (in lessons of 1 hour 24 minutes) DURATION MATHEMATICS Grade 1-3 343 344 Describe, compare and order numbers 1.4 CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS) Use ordinal numbers to show order, place or position Use, read and write ordinal numbers, including abbreviated form up to 31. Use, read and write ordinal numbers, including abbreviated form up to 31. Describe and order whole numbers up to 99 from smallest to greatest, and greatest to smallest • Describe and order whole numbers up to 999 from smallest to greatest, and greatest to smallest • Use ordinal numbers to show order, place or position Describe and compare whole numbers up to 99 using smaller than, greater than, more than, less than and is equal to • Write number names 0 - 500 • Write number names to 0 - 1 000 • Describe and compare whole numbers up to 999 using smaller than, greater than, more than, less than and is equal to Recognise, identify and reads number names 0 - 250 • Recognise, identify and read number names 0 - 1 000 • • Write number symbols 0 - 500 • Write number symbols 0 - 1 000 • Describe, compare and order numbers to 99 Recognise, identify and read number symbols 0 - 500 • Recognise, identify and read number symbols 0 - 1 000 • Describe, compare and order numbers to 999 Recognise, identify and read numbers Recognise, identify and read numbers 1.3 Number symbols and number names FOCUS FOR TERM 4 CONCEPTS AND SKILLS REQUIREMENT BY YEAR END CONCEPTS AND SKILLS TOPICS ordering and comparing numbers. • For working with ordinal numbers the calendar is ideal because it allows the learner to talk about the 23rd or the 31st day of the month. Learners need to practise reading and writing the abbreviated form. I know that and, and and . Also is three bundles of ten and is five bundles of ten. There are more bundles of ten in 50 than 30. is smaller than because: Example: During this term learners consolidate ordering and comparing numbers to and should be able to give reasons for why one number is bigger than another. Allow learners to use a number line, number track, number grids or even their knowledge of breaking up numbers into tens and ones to illustrate their understanding. When ordering numbers learners must be able to say why a number is bigger than another using the value of the digits to explain themselves. What is different from Grade 2? When writing three-digit numbers, the numbers between 100 and 110; 200 and 210; 300 and 310, 400 and 410, the digit in the tens position is zero. Some learners find it difficult to write these numbers in symbols when they are given the number in words. For example, it may be difficult for some learners to write ‘three hundred and four’ in symbols. They might write 3004. Place value cards are particularly useful for helping learners to understand how to represent these numbers correctly. Learners should also be given plenty of practice writing these numbers. Care should be taken to say numbers correctly; one needs to say 323 as “three hundred and twenty-three”, NOT as “323”. completing number sequences; and counting forwards and backwards; • • counting objects; • The reading and writing number symbols is also practised when: During this term learners recognise, read and write number symbols to . They read number names to and write number names to What is different from Grade 2?
